planting.note: Direct sow in fall in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial sun to full sun; coastal plain joe-pye weed tolerates periodic standing water and is ideal for rain gardens, bog margins, and low wet areas that challenge most perennials.

directions: Direct sow in fall in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial sun to full sun; coastal plain joe-pye weed tolerates periodic standing water and is ideal for rain gardens, bog margins, and low wet areas that challenge most perennials. Eutrochium dubium is a shorter and more compact species than the common E. purpureum, typically reaching 4–5 feet with distinctive whorled leaves in sets of three rather than four, and large, rounded, mauve-pink flower clusters from July through September that are among the best pollinator resources in the late-summer native garden, drawing monarch butterflies, swallowtails, and dozens of native bee species. It is native to Atlantic coastal plain wet meadows and swamps from Maine to Florida and is particularly well adapted to the heat and humidity of the Southeast.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; boneset is one of the few native perennials that grows happily in both seasonally flooded lowlands and ordinary moist borders.

directions: Direct sow in fall in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; boneset is one of the few native perennials that grows happily in both seasonally flooded lowlands and ordinary moist borders. Eupatorium perfoliatum is easily identified by its most unusual feature: pairs of opposite, lance-shaped leaves whose bases appear to completely fuse around the stem (perfoliate), as if the stem pierces through a single leaf; this trait led to the folk belief that the plant could "set" broken bones, hence the common name, and it was one of the most widely used medicinal plants of Native American and early American herbal medicine. Flat-topped clusters of white flowers bloom from late summer through fall, providing abundant nectar for monarchs, swallowtails, and countless native bees; the plant naturalizes well in moist native meadows, rain gardens, and riparian areas.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in dry to moderately moist, well-drained, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; brickellia is highly drought-tolerant once established and thrives in lean soils where most plants struggle.

directions: Direct sow in spring in dry to moderately moist, well-drained, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; brickellia is highly drought-tolerant once established and thrives in lean soils where most plants struggle. Brickellia grandiflora is a perennial subshrub native to open rocky slopes, canyon walls, and dry meadows throughout the western United States, producing aromatic, triangular, coarsely toothed leaves with pale undersides and numerous nodding, tassel-like heads of creamy-white flowers from July through October that are exceptional late-season nectar sources for migrating monarch butterflies and specialized native bees in the aster family. The plant is one of the most important native late-season nectar plants in western gardens and serves as an important floral resource when most other composites have finished blooming.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring (seeds need light to germinate) in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist soil in partial shade to full sun; do not cover seeds — simply press them firmly to the surface and keep moist.

directions: Surface-sow in spring (seeds need light to germinate) in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moist soil in partial shade to full sun; do not cover seeds — simply press them firmly to the surface and keep moist. Indian tobacco is a branching annual (sometimes biennial) native to eastern North American woodland edges and disturbed areas; it produces racemes of tiny, pale lavender-blue, two-lipped flowers throughout summer followed by inflated, balloon-like seed capsules (giving the species name inflata) that persist attractively into fall. All parts of the plant contain lobeline and related alkaloids that are highly toxic if ingested — historically it was used medicinally by indigenous peoples but requires expert knowledge; do not eat any part. The flowers provide nectar for small native bees; the plant self-seeds readily in suitable sites.

planting.note: Start transplants indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sidalcea tolerates light shade but blooms most prolifically in full sun.

directions: Start transplants indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost, or direct sow in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; sidalcea tolerates light shade but blooms most prolifically in full sun. Prairie checker mallow is a graceful western native perennial in the mallow family with deeply lobed, palmate leaves and slender, upright racemes of rosy-pink to lavender-pink, five-petaled flowers resembling small hollyhocks from late spring through midsummer; it is the primary larval host plant for the West Coast Lady butterfly (Vanessa annabella) and an important nectar source for native bumblebees and mining bees. Cut back after the first flush of bloom to encourage rebloom; plants are drought-tolerant once established and are exceptional in California-style meadow gardens and dry borders.

planting.note: Start transplants indoors 8 weeks before last frost or direct sow on the soil surface in late fall for spring germination; eastern smooth beardtongue adapts to moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light partial shade.

directions: Start transplants indoors 8 weeks before last frost or direct sow on the soil surface in late fall for spring germination; eastern smooth beardtongue adapts to moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light partial shade. Penstemon laevigatus is a southeastern native beardtongue that grows naturally along forest margins, roadsides, and moist open woods from Virginia to Florida and west to Louisiana; it produces large, white to pale lavender, tubular flowers with purple guidelines in the throat that are held on tall, upright racemes from late spring through early summer, providing nectar for ruby-throated hummingbirds, long-tongued bumblebees, and native carpenter bees. Unlike many western penstemons, it thrives in the humidity and summer rainfall of the Southeast; it is one of the most reliable and heat-tolerant penstemon species for Gulf Coast and mid-Atlantic gardens.
